text.title: VUI Inclusivity — Interaction Patterns
text.summary: Concrete dialogue and interaction patterns for inclusive voice interfaces: prompt design, error recovery, progressive disclosure, and accessibility benefit mapping.

example.code: "What would you like to do? You can say 'send message', 'check calendar', or 'play music'."
example.note: States purpose and gives three concrete examples of valid commands — covers discoverability without overwhelming.

antipattern.description: "What do you want?" with no examples or hints.
antipattern.reason: Users unfamiliar with the command grammar have no way to discover valid inputs and abandon the interface.

rule.statement: Start prompts minimal ("Say 'help' for options"), escalate to medium detail on first confusion, and offer full command lists only on explicit request.
rule.rationale: Dumping all available commands in the first prompt overwhelms users; progressive disclosure matches cognitive load to user need.

example.code: User: "Delete all emails" VUI: "I heard 'delete all emails'. This will delete 1,247 emails. Say 'confirm' to proceed or 'cancel' to stop."
example.note: Repeats what was heard, states the consequence, and requires explicit confirmation — prevents destructive actions from recognition errors.

rule.statement: Use a three-tier error recovery sequence: (1) simple retry prompt, (2) retry with example phrases, (3) handoff to visual interface or human agent.
rule.rationale: Three-tier recovery gives the system multiple chances to succeed while setting a clear escalation path that avoids infinite loops.

example.code:
Tier 1 (no input heard): "Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you say it again?"
Tier 2 (speech not matched): "I'm not sure what you mean. Try saying something like 'send a message' or 'set a reminder'."
Tier 3 (still failing): "Let me show you your options on screen."
example.note: Each tier adds information. Tier 3 switches modality rather than repeating the same failure path.

antipattern.description: Saying "I didn't understand" more than twice without offering new information or a different interaction path.
antipattern.reason: Repeating the same error message with no new guidance traps the user in a loop and damages trust.

rule.statement: Confirm every completed action across all available channels: voice response, visual indicator, haptic feedback, and audio cue — all conveying identical information.
rule.rationale: Redundant channels ensure confirmation reaches users regardless of sensory context or environmental interference.

example.code: Voice: "Message sent to John" Visual: Check mark + "Message sent" Haptic: Short confirmation vibration Audio: Success chime

list.item: Visual impairment: audio-first design, rich spoken feedback, no visual-only paths
Motor disabilities: voice-only paths for every feature, no required touch
Elderly users: patient response timing, simple command grammar, familiar metaphors
Non-native speakers: multi-language support, visual transcription confirmation
Cognitive disabilities: clear prompts, guided conversation flow, no time pressure
Dyslexia: spell out confirmations, read back entered text, no mandatory reading
Busy contexts (driving, cooking): eyes-free operation, interruption handling, short responses
